statement: Null safety in Kotlin is a design language, not just compiler protection. Types declare which absences are part of the domain and which are programming mistakes. The hidden payoff is social: teams argue less in comments and reviews because the API already states whether missing data is expected.

Structured concurrency makes cancellation and failure propagate through a tree on purpose. The hidden philosophy is that background work should have an owner. Coroutines feel lightweight, but Kotlin wants their lifetime to be explicit so abandoned work becomes a design smell instead of an operational surprise.

viewModelScope only cancels work it owns. The hidden failure is launching from a ViewModel into longer-lived scopes, holding UI references in collectors, or collecting hot streams forever without lifecycle-aware restart. Teams say they used viewModelScope when the real bug is that ownership escaped the ViewModel boundary.

Fragments fail less because their API is bad and more because they expose two overlapping lifecycles: fragment and view. The hidden landmine is keeping references, observers, or adapters alive past onDestroyView. Most random crashes are really stale view work running in a still-live fragment object.

Compose versus XML is a team operating model decision. Compose rewards state-driven UI, Kotlin fluency, and design iteration speed; XML still fits teams with heavy legacy views, tooling habits, or mixed libraries. The hidden mistake is migrating screen by screen without deciding where state and theming rules actually live.

Flow and LiveData overlap at the UI edge but differ in philosophy. Flow is a general coroutine stream abstraction with backpressure and operators; LiveData is lifecycle-aware state delivery for Android UI. The hidden rule is to do data work in Flow and adapt at the boundary rather than building new domain pipelines on LiveData.

Hilt matters less as syntax sugar and more as graph ownership. It gives Android apps a repeatable way to align object lifetime with application, activity, fragment, and ViewModel scopes. The hidden win is organizational: once scope is explicit, leaks and accidental singletons become reviewable design choices.

Gradle problems on Android are usually boundary problems disguised as build errors. Plugin order, version catalogs, AGP compatibility, and convention drift compound slowly until upgrades stall. The hidden rule is to centralize build logic early; duplicated module config feels fast until the first ecosystem-wide change lands.
